name: Pelias
description: Pelias was a king of Iolcus in Thessaly in Greek mythology. He was the son of Tyro and Poseidon, and usurped the throne from his half-brother Aeson, the rightful heir. Driven by ambition and a fear of a prophecy foretelling his demise at the hands of a man wearing only one sandal, Pelias set Jason, Aeson's son, on the quest for the Golden Fleece. This treacherous act was intended to remove Jason from Iolcus permanently. While Jason was away, Pelias continued to rule Iolcus with an iron fist, solidifying his power through tyranny and cruelty. Upon Jason's return with the Golden Fleece and Medea, Medea plotted revenge against Pelias for his unjust rule and the suffering he inflicted on his family. Through her sorcery, she convinced Pelias' daughters to murder him, promising to rejuvenate him afterward. However, Medea's promise was false, and Pelias met a gruesome end at the hands of his own daughters. His death marked the culmination of his ambition and treachery, demonstrating the consequences of defying fate and abusing power. He is a figure of ambition, fear, and the dangers of defying prophecy.
subtitle: The Usurper King of Iolcus
achievements: Sent Jason on the quest for the Golden Fleece

companions.name: Aeson
companions.description: Aeson was Pelias's half-brother and the rightful king of Iolcus, whom Pelias usurped. Pelias imprisoned Aeson to secure his claim to the throne.

companions.name: Tyro
companions.description: Tyro was the mother of Pelias through her union with Poseidon. She later suffered under Pelias's rule due to his ambition.

companions.name: Cretheus
companions.description: Cretheus was Tyro's husband and stepfather to Pelias. Although not closely associated with Pelias in myths, he represents the legitimate line of Iolcus that Pelias defied.

companions.name: Anaxibia
companions.description: Anaxibia (or Phylomache in some accounts) was the wife of Pelias, and the mother of his daughters. Her involvement in Pelias's death highlights the treacherous nature of his reign.

companions.name: Alcestis
companions.description: Alcestis was one of the daughters of Pelias, known for her willingness to sacrifice herself for her husband, Admetus. Though loyal, she was manipulated by Medea in the plot to rejuvenate Aeson, ultimately leading to Pelias's demise.

keyMyths.title: The Prophecy of the One-Sandaled Man
keyMyths.description: An oracle foretold Pelias' death at the hands of a man wearing only one sandal. This prophecy fueled his fear and led him to send Jason on the perilous quest for the Golden Fleece.

keyMyths.title: The Usurpation of the Throne
keyMyths.description: Pelias, born of Tyro and Poseidon, unjustly seized the throne of Iolcus from his half-brother Aeson. This act of treachery set the stage for the events that would lead to his downfall.

keyMyths.title: Medea's Revenge
keyMyths.description: Medea, fueled by her loyalty to Jason and her desire for revenge, tricked Pelias' daughters into murdering him with the promise of rejuvenation. This act brought about Pelias' demise and fulfilled the prophecy.

sources.author: Apollonius Rhodius
sources.description: Epic poem detailing the voyage of Jason and the Argonauts.
sources.text: Epic poem detailing the voyage of Jason and the Argonauts.

sources.author: Euripides
sources.description: Greek tragedy focusing on Medea's revenge against Jason and Pelias.
sources.text: Greek tragedy focusing on Medea's revenge against Jason and Pelias.

sources.author: Diodorus Siculus
sources.description: A universal history covering various mythological and historical events.
sources.text: A universal history covering various mythological and historical events.

worshipPractices.name: Libations to Poseidon
worshipPractices.description: As the son of Poseidon, Pelias may have been honored with libations, particularly water or wine, offered in gratitude for his lineage and perceived divine favor. These offerings would typically be made at altars dedicated to Poseidon or in areas associated with the sea and maritime activities.

festivals.name: Succession Celebrations
festivals.timing: After Pelias's usurpation of the throne
festivals.description: While not explicitly detailed, celebratory events likely occurred after Pelias seized power in Iolcus. These events would have involved feasting, athletic competitions, and sacrifices to the gods, all designed to solidify his claim to the throne and garner support from the populace.

cultural.modernLegacy: Pelias's story serves as a cautionary tale about ambition, betrayal, and the consequences of defying fate, often explored in literature and film. His role as the antagonist in the Jason and the Argonauts myth highlights themes of power struggles and the cyclical nature of vengeance that continue to resonate with audiences today.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Harald Fairhair, like Pelias, is known for seizing power and establishing his rule through ambition. Both figures faced challenges to their legitimacy and sought to consolidate their positions.

Like Pelias's fear of Jason and the one sandal, the Fomorians represent a constant threat and source of anxiety for the Tuatha Dé Danann. They symbolize the ever-present possibility of usurpation and chaos, similar to the threat Jason posed to Pelias's reign.
Set, like Pelias, is a figure who usurps power from his brother (Osiris). Both are motivated by ambition and a desire for kingship, leading to conflict and instability.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: As the son of Poseidon, Pelias is inherently connected to the sea and its power, reflecting his ambition and ruthlessness.

associations.name: Purple
associations.significance: Purple is associated with royalty and power, reflecting Pelias's position as a king, albeit a usurper.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: The serpent symbolizes deception and treachery, reflecting Pelias's manipulative nature and his usurpation of the throne.

associations.name: Usurpation
associations.significance: Pelias's defining act was seizing power illegitimately, making usurpation a central theme associated with him.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: The prophecy regarding the man with one sandal is intrinsically linked to Pelias's fate and paranoia.

associations.name: Fear
associations.significance: Pelias's actions are largely driven by fear of the prophecy, making fear a key element of his character.

associations.name: Nightshade
associations.significance: Nightshade is poisonous, representing the dangerous and ultimately fatal consequences of Pelias's actions and character.

quests.title: The Golden Fleece: Pelias's Scheme
quests.description: Pelias, fearing Jason's return, sends him on a seemingly impossible quest to retrieve the Golden Fleece from Colchis. He hopes Jason will die in the attempt, securing Pelias's reign.
companions: Argonauts (including Heracles, Orpheus, Castor, and Pollux)
quests.outcome: Jason, with the help of Medea, successfully retrieves the Golden Fleece and returns to Iolcus, though not without great loss and challenges.

quests.title: The Test of the Sandal
quests.description: Pelias receives a prophecy that a man wearing only one sandal will cause his downfall. When Jason arrives at Iolcus wearing only one sandal, having lost the other crossing a river, Pelias recognizes him as the prophesied threat.
quests.outcome: Pelias recognizes Jason as the fulfillment of the prophecy, fueling his fear and driving him to desperate measures to maintain his power.

quests.title: Deception and the Daughters of Pelias
quests.description: Medea, seeking revenge on Pelias for his treachery, convinces his daughters that she can restore his youth by cutting him into pieces and boiling him in a potion. She demonstrates this on an old ram, seemingly rejuvenating it.
quests.outcome: Pelias's daughters, deceived by Medea, kill their father, hoping to restore him to youth. Medea then flees, leaving Iolcus in chaos and fulfilling her vengeance.

feats.title: Usurpation of the Throne
feats.description: Pelias, driven by ambition, forcefully seizes the throne of Iolcus from his half-brother Aeson, the rightful king. He effectively imprisons Aeson and threatens his family.
feats.significance: This act establishes Pelias as a ruthless and power-hungry ruler, setting the stage for the conflicts and tragedies that follow.

feats.title: The Construction of a False Pretense
feats.description: Pelias feigns willingness to cede the throne to Jason, but only after Jason retrieves the Golden Fleece. This is a calculated move to rid himself of a perceived threat.
feats.significance: This demonstrates Pelias's cunning and manipulation, highlighting his willingness to use deceit to achieve his goals and protect his power.

feats.title: Rule of Iolcus
feats.description: Pelias ruled Iolcus with strength and established himself as a king, even if through illegitimate means. He managed to maintain his power for a significant period, despite the prophecy hanging over his head.
feats.significance: He became a symbol of unlawful power and the consequences of fear driven rule.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Usurpation of Iolcus
extendedContent.content: Pelias's birth was shrouded in divine intervention and ambition. His mother, Tyro, was the daughter of Salmoneus and possessed great beauty. She was relentlessly pursued by Poseidon, the god of the sea, who disguised himself as the river god Enipeus to seduce her. From this union, Pelias and his twin brother Neleus were born. Tyro, ashamed of the illegitimate birth, abandoned them. They were discovered and raised by a herdsman. As they matured, Pelias displayed a ruthless ambition that contrasted sharply with Neleus's more moderate temperament. Learning of their royal lineage and Aeson's right to the throne of Iolcus, Pelias took it upon himself to seize power. He imprisoned Aeson and effectively usurped the kingship, exiling Neleus in the process. This act of treachery set the stage for the events that would define his reign and ultimately lead to his downfall. The details surrounding the usurpation vary in accounts, but the core element of Pelias's ambition and willingness to betray family for power remains consistent. His actions established him as a figure defined by his ruthless pursuit of authority.

extendedContent.title: The Prophecy and the Quest for the Golden Fleece
extendedContent.content: Pelias's reign was perpetually haunted by a prophecy that foretold his death at the hands of a man wearing only one sandal. This prophecy, delivered by an oracle, instilled a deep-seated fear in the king. When Jason, Aeson's son, arrived in Iolcus to reclaim his birthright, he lost a sandal while crossing a river. This immediately triggered Pelias's fear, recognizing Jason as the man prophesied to end his reign. To rid himself of this perceived threat, Pelias devised a cunning and treacherous plan: he tasked Jason with retrieving the Golden Fleece from Colchis, a seemingly impossible feat intended to lead to Jason's demise. The Fleece, guarded by a dragon and located in a distant land, presented an insurmountable challenge. Pelias believed that Jason would either die during the journey or be unable to complete the task. This act demonstrates Pelias's manipulative nature and his willingness to sacrifice others to preserve his own power. The Quest for the Golden Fleece, while attributed to Jason's heroism, was fundamentally orchestrated by Pelias's fear and desire for self-preservation.

extendedContent.title: Medea's Deception and the Death of Pelias
extendedContent.content: Upon Jason's triumphant return with the Golden Fleece, Pelias remained unwilling to relinquish the throne. Medea, Jason's wife and a powerful sorceress, arrived in Iolcus, feigning friendship and offering Pelias a promise of renewed youth. She demonstrated her powers by rejuvenating an old ram, convincing Pelias's daughters that she could do the same for him. Medea instructed Pelias's daughters to cut him into pieces and boil him in a cauldron with specific herbs. However, she deliberately omitted the crucial rejuvenating herbs, resulting in Pelias's gruesome death. This act of deception and revenge highlights Medea's cunning and her unwavering loyalty to Jason. The story of Pelias's death serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of ambition, treachery, and the allure of false promises. It also underscores the power of revenge and the consequences of underestimating one's enemies. Medea's manipulation of Pelias's daughters and the brutal nature of his death emphasize the themes of betrayal and the destructive nature of unchecked ambition.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Cultural Significance
extendedContent.content: Pelias's legacy is primarily defined by his role as the antagonist in the legend of Jason and the Argonauts. He represents the archetype of the power-hungry and treacherous ruler, willing to sacrifice anything to maintain his authority. While he is not remembered for any heroic deeds or positive contributions, his actions set in motion one of the most famous quests in Greek mythology. His fear of the prophecy and his manipulation of Jason ultimately led to his own demise, highlighting the themes of fate and the consequences of hubris. Pelias's story serves as a reminder that unchecked ambition and treachery often lead to downfall. Although Pelias himself is not a sympathetic character, his story is a crucial element of the larger narrative of Jason and the Argonauts, contributing to the enduring popularity and cultural significance of the myth. His actions serve as a foil to Jason's heroism, emphasizing the importance of courage, loyalty, and justice.

shortDescription: Pelias was a king of Iolcus who seized the throne from his half-brother. He sent Jason on the quest for the Golden Fleece, hoping to be rid of him.
